nativeRange.habitat: The Whiptail Catfish inhabits fast-moving, well-oxygenated rivers and streams draining both the Pacific and Caribbean slopes of its range. It is found over rocky substrates where biofilm — a layer of algae, diatoms, bacteria, and microorganisms — accumulates on exposed rock and wood surfaces. Water is typically clear to slightly turbid with consistent, strong flow.

decorPreferences: smooth or rounded river rocks for aufwuchs grazing surfaces
driftwood pieces (also provide dietary biofilm and fiber)
flat stones or slate as potential spawning sites
moderate to strong flow (powerhead or wavemaker recommended)
open substrate areas with fine sand or smooth gravel
minimal sharp decor — long filaments and fin extensions are fragile

diet.notes: The Whiptail Catfish is a dedicated grazer of aufwuchs — the thin layer of algae, diatoms, and microorganisms that coats hard surfaces — and rasps biofilm from rocks, wood, and glass throughout the day. Supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, courgette), and Repashy gel foods (herbivore or soilent green formulas). Occasional offerings of frozen Artemia or daphnia may be accepted but plant matter must form the bulk of the diet. Driftwood nibbling is normal and beneficial. Do not rely on tank algae alone — dedicated supplemental feeding is essential for long-term health.

goodWith: tetras and other small to mid-sized characins
other non-aggressive loricariids of different size
avoidWith: aggressive or territorial cichlids that may harass bottom dwellers
very small nano fish that may be accidentally disrupted
other Sturisoma males (territorial disputes at spawning time)

imageCandidates.notes: Carlos Ceballos, 2048x945. Sharp full lateral of a live wild individual on natural mossy substrate; clearly shows the diagnostic elongated whip-like body, pointed rostral snout, erect dorsal fin, pectoral fins, and trailing caudal filament with the natural golden-brown mottled coloration. No watermark.

Carlos Ceballos, 1536x2048. In-hand dorsal view dramatically illustrating the long trailing whip caudal filaments and rostral snout; complements the lateral shot with a different angle emphasizing the namesake 'whiptail' feature.
Carlos Ceballos, 2048x1536. Lateral profile of a live specimen showing bony body armor plating and head/rostrum detail; different individual for variety.

summary: The Whiptail Catfish (Sturisoma panamense) is a slender, elongated armored catfish from fast-flowing rivers in Panama and Colombia, recognizable by the long fin and facial filaments that develop on mature males. It grazes constantly on biofilm and algae and needs daily supplemental vegetable foods and strong water movement. Males guard and fan the egg clutch after spawning.
careNotes:
The Panama Whiptail Catfish (Sturisoma panamense, sometimes listed in the hobby as Sturisomatichthys panamensis following a generic revision) originates from well-oxygenated rivers draining the Pacific and Caribbean slopes of Panama and Colombia — including the Santa María, Tuira, Atrato, Sinú, and Truandó drainages. Wild fish inhabit rocky, fast-flowing reaches where biofilm (aufwuchs) — a community of algae, diatoms, bacteria, and microinvertebrates — is constantly renewed on rock and wood surfaces.

Tank setup should replicate good flow: a powerhead, wavemaker, or strong canister outlet directed along the tank length is strongly recommended. Provide multiple smooth rocks, slate slabs, and driftwood pieces that accumulate biofilm. Whiptail catfish are not nocturnal in the strict sense and will graze actively throughout the day under normal lighting, making them an especially visible and engaging community resident. Their long, delicate rostral filaments (rostral odontodes on mature males) and caudal filaments are prone to damage from sharp decor or aggressive tankmates — avoid both.

Diet is the most critical care aspect. These fish are genuine herbivores and constant grazers; they should be fed multiple times daily with algae wafers, spirulina flakes, blanched zucchini or cucumber (left weighted to the bottom), and Repashy Soilent Green or similar gel food. Rasping driftwood is normal and beneficial. Starvation is the most common cause of decline — sunken belly is an early warning sign requiring immediate dietary intervention. Supplemental high-protein foods are rarely needed and excess protein can cause digestive issues.

Water temperature should remain in the 20–26°C range; temperatures above 27°C are stressful long-term. The genus Sturisomatichthys is a facultative air breather, capable of gulping air from the surface when dissolved oxygen is low, but good oxygenation through surface agitation should be maintained regardless. The species is listed as Least Concern (IUCN, assessed 2019/2020).

A note on taxonomy: the species described by Eigenmann and Eigenmann in 1889 as Sturisoma panamense has been placed by some authors in the separate genus Sturisomatichthys, where it appears under the name S. panamensis. Both names remain in use in trade and hobbyist literature.
breedingNotes:
The Panama Whiptail is a substrate spawner and a reliable breeder in well-maintained aquaria, rated moderate difficulty primarily due to its size requirements and the need for stable, good water quality. Condition a pair or trio (one male, one to two females) with generous vegetable feeding. Spawning can be encouraged by a 20–30% water change with slightly cooler, well-oxygenated water and increased flow.

The female deposits a compact batch of approximately 80–120 orange-yellow eggs directly onto a flat surface — commonly a flat stone, piece of slate, driftwood, or the aquarium glass. After fertilization the male takes sole responsibility for guarding and fanning the clutch, which he does continuously for 7–10 days until hatching. The male should not be disturbed during this period. Females and other tankmates are generally ignored by the guarding male but may be moved to a separate tank if harassed.

Fry are relatively large at hatching and will begin grazing on biofilm and soft algae within a day or two. Supplement with powdered spirulina, finely grated blanched vegetables, and infusoria or micro-algae paste for the first two weeks. Fry growth is moderate and they become recognizable miniatures of adults within 4–6 weeks. Pair bonding is loosely maintained across multiple spawning cycles.

primary.name: FishBase — Sturisomatichthys panamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided: adult size (26.0 cm SL male/unsexed), temperature (20–22°C), pH (6.5–7.2), hardness (up to 25 dH), native range (Pacific slope rivers of Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador; Caribbean slope rivers of Colombia), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, subfamily Loricariinae), trophic level 2.4 (herbivorous/detritivorous), facultative air-breathing in genus, IUCN Least Concern (assessed November 2019), reproductive notes (female deposits ~100 eggs on plants or glass; male guards clutch). Temperature upper bound extended to 26°C for aquarium husbandry based on hobbyist consensus across the genus.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sturisomatichthys panamensis
additional.notes: Confirmed geographic range: Santa María and Tuira River drainages (Pacific slope, Panama) eastward to Atrato, Sinú, and Truandó drainages (Atlantic/Caribbean slope, Colombia). Maximum standard length 26 cm. Described by C.H. Eigenmann and R.S. Eigenmann in 1889. IUCN Least Concern (2020). Facultative air-breathing in Sturisomatichthys genus confirmed.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bristlenose Pleco Care Guide (loricariid husbandry reference)
additional.notes: Used as Aquarium Co-Op loricariid care reference in the absence of a dedicated Sturisoma species page on the site. Informed general husbandry guidance: temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.5–7.8, importance of supplemental vegetable foods beyond tank algae, hide/cover needs, peaceful community compatibility, and nitrate maintenance below 40 ppm.

additional.name: Wikimedia Commons — Sturisoma panamense image
additional.notes: Image source verification for imageCandidates. Lateral specimen photograph reviewed as primary image candidate.

fish.breedingNotes: Substrate spawner; female deposits 80–120 eggs on flat surface; male guards and fans clutch for 7–10 days until hatching; fry graze biofilm immediately; multiple spawning cycles with same pair.
